_scope_note: Captures the hyper bands (apparent-velocity model), translation mechanics, and the hyper-limit table. DEFERRED for now (per plan): gravity waves, the Tellerman wave, grav-wave sail riding, and dimensional shear beyond the basic hyper-limit rule.

bands.name: Alpha

apparent_velocity_c.method: Interpolated. Geometric extrapolation downward (~1.65x per band) from the canon Delta/Epsilon/Zeta apparent velocities.

bands.typical_users: The main/lowest travel band; historically the only one used until the hyper log improved.
bands.danger: Entry above 0.3c is instantly fatal — a limit that took ~200 years to be accepted.

bands.name: Beta

apparent_velocity_c.method: Interpolated (no canon speed given for Beta). Geometric, between Alpha and Gamma.

bands.notes: No canon speed figures; one of the 'interpolate now' bands.

bands.name: Gamma

apparent_velocity_c.method: Interpolated. Geometric, between Beta and the canon Delta value.

bands.typical_users: A low travel band. Grayson hyper-generator tech was limited to the middle Gamma band before it joined the Manticoran Alliance.

apparent_velocity_c.method: Canon apparent velocity (at 0.5c real, low Delta). Used directly as the band nominal.

canon_datapoints.note: Older Manticoran merchant ships, lower end of Delta.

apparent_velocity_c.method: Canon apparent velocity (at 0.7c real). Used directly as the band nominal.

canon_datapoints.note: ~32% faster than the average merchant; few merchant ships go this fast.

bands.name: Zeta

apparent_velocity_c.method: Canon apparent velocity (at 0.7c real). Used directly as the band nominal.

bands.typical_users: Frequently used by warships — good speed with moderate safety.

bands.name: Eta

apparent_velocity_c.method: Interpolated/extrapolated (no canon speed given for Eta). Geometric, ~1.65x above Zeta and below Theta. THE primary 'interpolate now' band.

bands.notes: No canon speed figures; value invented to keep the band sequence monotonic and usable.

bands.name: Theta

apparent_velocity_c.method: Extrapolated (~1.65x above Eta). Canon gives only a relative hint (couriers on the 'ragged edge' run ~40% faster than frigates cruising Zeta), which does not cap the band ceiling.

bands.typical_users: The fastest band in normal use; couriers ride its 'ragged edge'.
bands.danger: Highest band ever used and the most dangerous to travel.

bands.name: Iota

apparent_velocity_c.method: Extrapolated (~1.65x above Theta). Invented; treat as indicative only.

bands.typical_users: Effectively inaccessible until the early 20th Century PD — no ship entered and returned before then.

bands.name: Kappa

apparent_velocity_c.method: Extrapolated (~1.65x above Iota). Invented; treat as indicative only.

bands.typical_users: Only became reachable with the streak drive in the early 20th Century PD.

translate_up.description: Crossing UP a band boundary bleeds off a percentage of relative velocity (regained by re-accelerating once in the new band) and crosses dangerous gravity-wave surges at the boundary. The Warshawski sail negated both the speed-bleed penalty and the shear danger.

translate_down.description: Each downward translation slows the ship to one-fifth of its relative velocity. Hardware faces no shear danger going down, but crew suffer nausea/distress that worsens with speed.
translate_down.worked_example: A ship at ~0.3c (90,000 km/s) translating down four bands (Delta -> n-space) ends at 90,000 / 5^4 = 144 km/s without re-acceleration.

crash_translation.description: A high-velocity translation: faster but induces strong nausea/physical distress; naval crews train for it, but it is avoided unless necessary. Merchants prefer low-velocity translations to reduce alpha-node wear and crew distress.

max_velocity_c.notes: NOT stated on the hyperspace/hyper-limit pages. ~0.8c is the widely-cited particle-shielding ceiling for impeller-wedge ships; the simulator's existing examples use 0.6c for a merchant. Confirm against a primary source before treating as canon. Typical real velocities seen in canon hyper travel are 0.5-0.7c.

hyper_limit.source: UHH; Pearls of Weber (davidweber.net post 16, hyperlimits by class)
hyper_limit.description: Radius around a star inside which hyper translation causes fatal gravimetric shear. A ship must be BEYOND this limit to translate into or out of hyperspace. This sets the in-system distance a ship must cross under impeller drive before it can hyper out (departure leg) and where it re-enters n-space inbound.
anomalies: F1 (25.98) exceeds F0 (25.42), breaking the otherwise-monotonic decline from hotter to cooler stars — almost certainly a transcription typo in the source table (F0 likely ~26.4, or F1 ~24.98). Values are captured verbatim from canon; do not silently 'correct' them, but be aware when interpolating around F0-F1.
F5 (24.10) is slightly off the steady ~0.44 lm/subclass cadence seen across F2-F9 and all of G/K/M; minor rounding in the source.
hyper_limit.interpolation_note: O, B, and A are given as single representative values (no subclasses). For an O/B/A subclass, interpolate linearly toward the next listed anchor (A -> F0). White dwarfs, giants (other than the one 'Red Giant' entry), and supergiants are not tabulated; invent as needed and flag canon:false. F/G/K/M subclasses are fully tabulated and need no interpolation.

applied_examples._note: Looked up from by_spectral_class for our built systems (and Sol). The lookup is canon-exact, so these are canon values, not fabrications.
